turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Breyer has our opinion this morning in case 17-1705, PDR Network v. Carlton & Harris Chiropractic.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: There are several federal statutes known as the Hobbs Act, this one is quite technical and has to do with regulatory agencies, and it says that the final order, for example, the Federal Communications Commission, that the Court of Appeals, Federal Court of Appeals will have 'exclusive jurisdiction' to enjoin, set aside, suspend or determine the validity of the order. In this case, a District Court wanted to determine the validity of the order and the Court of Appeals for the Fourth Circuit said no, as the Court of Appeals, you have to take the order as given. Well, the case is more difficult than my summary made it sound and what we are doing is sending it back, because we think we cannot answer the question until the Court of Appeals decides two preliminary matters. The first matter is what kind of an order is this? It is a rule, but is it a legislative rule, which is just like law or is it an interpretive rule, which is just the FCC's idea of a law, of what the statute means, it is not a law itself automatically, and we want them to answer that because we think that if it is an interpretive rule, maybe, we are not saying definitely, the District Court could go ahead. And then the next question we want them to answer is whether the party here, the PDR Network, had a 'prior and adequate opportunity' to actually go to the Court of Appeals when the first rule came out, did they have a chance to get the thing reviewed at all, because if they did not, then they have a stronger claim that the District Court should review it now. Again, we do not say anything definitely. All we do is we send the case back and we ask the Court of Appeals to decide those two preliminary questions and then maybe eventually we will answer the question that the parties wanted us to answer. Actually, we did not even agree on that. There is a majority for it and that majority vacates the judgment, sends it back to determine they consider these issues. Justice Thomas has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment, in which Justice Gorsuch joins. Justice Kavanaugh has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment, in which Justice Thomas, Justice Alito and Justice Gorsuch join.
